resume:
Director of Product — B2B payments platform

Flowpay — Director of Product (2020-present)
- Situation: merchant onboarding took 9 business days median; 31% of applicants abandoned before activation.
- Task: own a cross-functional effort to cut time-to-activation and reduce abandonment without loosening risk controls.
- Action: re-sequenced KYB verification to run async, introduced a progressive-disclosure application flow, and partnered with risk to auto-approve low-risk segments.
- Result: median onboarding dropped from 9 days to 1.5 days; abandonment fell from 31% to 12%; activated-merchant volume up 22% YoY.

Flowpay — Group Product Manager (2017-2020)
- Launched the dispute-management product line from 0 to $4M ARR.

Northgate Financial — Senior PM (2014-2017)

Skills: payments, risk/KYB, experimentation, roadmap prioritization, P&L ownership.
jd: Flowpay is hiring a Director of Product to own the merchant-acquisition surface end to end: onboarding, activation, and the risk/growth balance that governs both. You will be accountable for time-to-activation, activation-rate, and fraud-loss-basis-points as a joint scorecard with the risk org — these goals are in deliberate tension, and we want a leader who can articulate the tradeoff explicitly rather than optimizing one at the expense of the other. You will lead a triad with engineering and design, set the quarterly roadmap, and defend prioritization calls to the exec team. We expect crisp storytelling: the situation you inherited, the decision you made, the action you drove, and—critically—how you measured the result and what it cost on the other side of the ledger.

history.q: Walk me through the onboarding-time win on your resume — the situation, what you decided to do, and how it turned out, including the risk side of the ledger.

candidate_last_answer: Sure. When I took over the acquisition surface, onboarding was a mess — 9-day median, almost a third of applicants dropping off before they ever activated. Risk and growth were basically at war. So I framed it as a joint problem and got both orgs to commit to a shared scorecard. The mandate was clear: cut time-to-activation and abandonment, but not by loosening controls. I personally drove the strategy — re-sequencing KYB to run async, pushing a progressive-disclosure flow, and getting risk to agree to auto-approve the clearly low-risk segments. I spent a lot of political capital getting the risk VP to sign off on that last one; that was the hard-won decision. We aligned the triad, set the quarterly bets, and shipped it in two phases.

top_question_traits: must-anchor-on: We aligned the triad, set the quarterly bets, and shipped it in two phases

expected-yield: the actual result numbers the answer omitted — final time-to-activation, abandonment, and especially the fraud-loss-basis-points the auto-approval cost, closing the missing R of the STAR story
